R4MS - Workshop 2 Final Exercise

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author

Katie Dyck

Published

June 4, 2026

1 Introduction

Here we will be wrangling messy data files on water quality and predatory fish species from the Ross River estuary fish surveys. We know that there are 4 survey sites, 4 species, and 30 days of sampling. One of the files contains metadata on the sites and sensor data on the environmental conditions at each site.

2 Housekeeping

Code 
rm(list = ls())

3 Packages

Code 
library(tidyverse)  # includes stringr, lubridate, ggplot2, dplyr, purrr, etc.
library(readxl)     # for reading .xlsx files

4 Loading and Cleaning the data

4.1 Load catch data

The catch data is divided across 4 sheets in one excel file.

Load sheets individually:

Code 
catch1 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 1)
catch2 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 2)
catch3 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 3)
catch4 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 4)

4.2 Inspect individual sheets

Code 
print(catch1)
# A tibble: 104 × 4
   site       date                species       count
   <chr>      <dttm>              <chr>         <dbl>
 1 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi        6
 2 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     3
 3 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 bream             4
 4 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead          2
 5 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 barramundi        1
 6 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     1

 8 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathead          3
 9 Lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     2
10 Lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 flathead          3
# ℹ 94 more rows

print(catch2)

   site     date                species       count

 4 Mid_Ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead          3
 5 Mid_Ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     1

 7 Mid_Ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathead          3

10 Mid_Ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 bream             1
# ℹ 96 more rows

print(catch3)
# A tibble: 107 × 4
   site       date                species       count
   <chr>      <dttm>              <chr>         <dbl>
 1 ROSS MOUTH 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi        3

 3 ROSS MOUTH 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead          9

 7 ROSS MOUTH 2026-05-03 00:00:00 barramundi        1

 9 ROSS MOUTH 2026-05-03 00:00:00 bream             2
10 ROSS MOUTH 2026-05-03 00:00:00 flathead          7
# ℹ 97 more rows
Code 
print(catch4)
# A tibble: 110 × 4
   site       date                species       count
   <chr>      <dttm>              <chr>         <dbl>
 1 Upper Ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi        5
 2 Upper Ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 bream             2
 3 Upper Ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead          6
 4 Upper Ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 barramundi        8
 5 Upper Ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     1
 6 Upper Ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 bream             2
 7 Upper Ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathead          4

10 Upper Ross 2026-05-04 00:00:00 barramundi        5
# ℹ 100 more rows

There are variations in the species and site names, but they otherwise look to be the same. We can clean after binding all the rows together.

4.3 Bind rows together

Code 
catch_data <- bind_rows(catch1, catch2, catch3, catch4)
# Clean up the environment
rm(catch1, catch2, catch3, catch4)

4.4 Examine catch data

Code 
print(catch_data)
# A tibble: 427 × 4
   site       date                species       count
   <chr>      <dttm>              <chr>         <dbl>
 1 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barramundi        6
 2 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     3
 3 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 bream             4
 4 Lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathead          2
 5 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 barramundi        1
 6 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     1
 7 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 bream             3
 8 Lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathead          3
 9 Lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 MANGROVE JACK     2
10 Lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 flathead          3
# ℹ 417 more rows

We want to know how many levels of site and species we have, which we can’t tell how many there are from these outputs. One option is to check these columns explicitly using distinct():

Code 
catch_data |> 
  distinct(site)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site      
  <chr>     
1 Lower_ross
2 Mid_Ross  
3 ROSS MOUTH
4 Upper Ross
Code 
catch_data |> 
  distinct(species)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  species      
  <chr>        
1 barramundi   
2 MANGROVE JACK
3 bream        
4 flathead

4.4.1 Next steps for catch data

We need the site names to have a uniform format. It looks like sites correspond to unique zones on the Ross River, but we need to load the metadata to confirm this. We can use the stringr package to make site names consistent so we can then load the taxonomic dictionary file to translate common to scientific species names.

4.5 Load metadata

Rows: 4
Columns: 4
$ site_name <chr> "Upper_Ross", "mid_ross", "LOWER ROSS", "ross_mouth"
$ lat       <dbl> -19.31, -19.28, -19.26, -19.25
$ lon       <dbl> 146.74, 146.78, 146.81, 146.83
$ zone      <chr> "Upstream", "Middle", "Downstream", "Marine"

4.6 Clean metadata

Code 
metadata |> 
  distinct(site_name)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site_name 
  <chr>     
1 Upper_Ross
2 mid_ross  
3 LOWER ROSS
4 ross_mouth

Site names in metadata are all over the place. We need to standardize them so we can join this table with the catch data table.

4.6.1 Standardize site names in catch data and metadata

Aside from simply standardizing the site names, we also want to convert the character variables to factors.

Code 
catch_data <- catch_data |> 
  mutate(
        # Replace spaces with underscores
        site = str_replace_all(site, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
        # Change text to lowercase
        site = str_to_lower(site)
    ) |> 
  # Convert all character to factor
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))
Code 
metadata <- metadata |> 
  mutate(
        # Replace spaces with underscores
        site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
        # Change text to lowercase
        site_name = str_to_lower(site_name)
    ) |> 
  # Convert all character to factor
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))

4.6.2 Recheck site names

Code 
catch_data |> 
  distinct(site)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site      
  <fct>     
1 lower_ross
2 mid_ross  
3 ross_mouth
4 upper_ross
Code 
metadata |> 
  distinct(site_name)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  site_name 
  <fct>     
1 upper_ross
2 mid_ross  
3 lower_ross
4 ross_mouth

Site names are now standardized across the catch data and metadata files.

4.7 Load taxonomic dictionary

Rows: 4
Columns: 2
$ common_name     <chr> "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "bream", "flathead"
$ scientific_name <chr> "Lates calcarifer", "Lutjanus argentimaculatus", "Acan…

4.8 Standardize species (common) names in catch data

The common species names are already in a standardized format. We need to standardize the species names in the catch data to match.

Code 
catch_data <- catch_data |>
  mutate(
    species = str_to_lower(str_trim(species)),
    species = str_replace_all(species, pattern = " ", replacement = "_")
  )

catch_data |> 
  distinct(species)
# A tibble: 4 × 1
  species      
  <chr>        
1 barramundi   
2 mangrove_jack
3 bream        
4 flathead

4.8.1 Create new variable (“scientific_name”) in catch log by joining with taxonomic dictionary

Code 
catch_data <- catch_data |>
  mutate(common_name = species) |>
  left_join(dictionary, by = join_by(common_name))
Code 
glimpse(catch_data)
Rows: 427
Columns: 6
$ site            <fct> lower_ross, lower_ross, lower_ross, lower_ross, lower_…
$ date            <dttm> 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-05-01, 2026-…
$ species         <chr> "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "bream", "flathead", "b…
$ count           <dbl> 6, 3, 4, 2, 1, 1, 3, 3, 2, 3, 1, 8, 4, 1, 1, 2, 1, 2, …
$ common_name     <chr> "barramundi", "mangrove_jack", "bream", "flathead", "b…
$ scientific_name <chr> "Lates calcarifer", "Lutjanus argentimaculatus", "Acan…

Looks good - the species and site names are fixed.

4.9 Load and wrangle sensor data

spc_tbl_ [11,520 × 5] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)
 $ site       : chr [1:11520] "upper_ross" "mid_ross" "lower_ross" "ross_mouth" ...
 $ timestamp  : chr [1:11520] "01/05/2026 00:00" "01/05/2026 00:00" "01/05/2026 00:00" "01/05/2026 00:00" ...
 $ temperature: num [1:11520] 23.7 24.1 24.2 24.6 26.1 ...
 $ salinity   : num [1:11520] 4.05 15.74 28.93 33.89 3.96 ...
 $ turbidity  : num [1:11520] 6.49 49.22 48.14 49.81 25.18 ...
 - attr(*, "spec")=
  .. cols(
  ..   site = col_character(),
  ..   timestamp = col_character(),
  ..   temperature = col_double(),
  ..   salinity = col_double(),
  ..   turbidity = col_double()
  .. )
 - attr(*, "problems")=<externalptr>
  • Sonde data was collected every 15 minutes for the same 4 sites across the 30 days of sampling. The data was sourced in a daily format, and outliers were removed.
    • timestamp is a character-type column. We will probably want to parse this to a date or date-time column.
  • There are three different environmental variables measured: temperature, salinity, and turbidity.
    • Environmental variables need cleaning: some negative salinity values, some -999 turbidity values (failed values).
  • Could investigate effects of all three on fish spp abundance or pick one? Need to think about prior research and hypotheses etc.

4.10 Wrangle sensor data

Code 
sensor_data <- sensor_raw |> 
    mutate(
      # Parse the character string to Day/Month/Year Hour:Minute
      datetime = dmy_hm(timestamp),
      # Chance -999.0 to NA values
      turbidity = na_if(turbidity, -999),
      # Convert negative salinity values to NA
      salinity = ifelse(salinity < 0, NA, salinity)
    )

print(sensor_data)
# A tibble: 11,520 × 6
   site       timestamp       temperature salinity turbidity datetime           
   <chr>      <chr>                 <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dttm>             
 1 upper_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        23.7     4.05      6.49 2026-05-01 00:00:00
 2 mid_ross   01/05/2026 00:…        24.1    15.7      49.2  2026-05-01 00:00:00
 3 lower_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        24.2    28.9      48.1  2026-05-01 00:00:00
 4 ross_mouth 01/05/2026 00:…        24.6    33.9      49.8  2026-05-01 00:00:00
 5 upper_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        26.1     3.96     25.2  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 6 mid_ross   01/05/2026 00:…        24.6    12.1      34.8  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 7 lower_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        22.9    30.9      33.9  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 8 ross_mouth 01/05/2026 00:…        20.6    33.9      27.4  2026-05-01 00:15:00
 9 upper_ross 01/05/2026 00:…        24.9     4.32     13.0  2026-05-01 00:30:00
10 mid_ross   01/05/2026 00:…        24.3    17.4      31.9  2026-05-01 00:30:00
# ℹ 11,510 more rows

4.10.1 Summarize 15-minute sensor data into daily means

Code 
sensor_daily <- sensor_data |>
  mutate(date = as_date(floor_date(datetime, "day"))) |>
  group_by(site, date) |>
  summarise(
    mean_temp = mean(temperature, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_salinity = mean(salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_turbidity = mean(turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop")

print(sensor_daily)
# A tibble: 120 × 5
   site       date       mean_temp mean_salinity mean_turbidity
   <chr>      <date>         <dbl>         <dbl>          <dbl>
 1 lower_ross 2026-05-01      23.8          28.4           27.9
 2 lower_ross 2026-05-02      24.0          27.7           24.1
 3 lower_ross 2026-05-03      24.2          28.1           20.6
 4 lower_ross 2026-05-04      24.0          28.2           28.4
 5 lower_ross 2026-05-05      23.9          27.6           26.7
 6 lower_ross 2026-05-06      24.0          28.2           24.0
 7 lower_ross 2026-05-07      24.0          27.6           25.1
 8 lower_ross 2026-05-08      24.0          28.0           25.9
 9 lower_ross 2026-05-09      24.0          27.8           24.6
10 lower_ross 2026-05-10      23.9          28.1           26.2
# ℹ 110 more rows

5 Join catch data with metadata and sensor data

Code 
catch_data_final <- catch_data |> 
  left_join(metadata, by = join_by(site == site_name)) |> 
  left_join(sensor_daily, by = join_by(site, date))

print(catch_data_final)
# A tibble: 427 × 12
   site      date                species count common_name scientific_name   lat
   <chr>     <dttm>              <chr>   <dbl> <chr>       <chr>           <dbl>
 1 lower_ro… 2026-05-01 00:00:00 barram…     6 barramundi  Lates calcarif… -19.3
 2 lower_ro… 2026-05-01 00:00:00 mangro…     3 mangrove_j… Lutjanus argen… -19.3
 3 lower_ro… 2026-05-01 00:00:00 bream       4 bream       Acanthopagrus … -19.3
 4 lower_ro… 2026-05-01 00:00:00 flathe…     2 flathead    Platycephalus … -19.3
 5 lower_ro… 2026-05-02 00:00:00 barram…     1 barramundi  Lates calcarif… -19.3
 6 lower_ro… 2026-05-02 00:00:00 mangro…     1 mangrove_j… Lutjanus argen… -19.3
 7 lower_ro… 2026-05-02 00:00:00 bream       3 bream       Acanthopagrus … -19.3
 8 lower_ro… 2026-05-02 00:00:00 flathe…     3 flathead    Platycephalus … -19.3
 9 lower_ro… 2026-05-03 00:00:00 mangro…     2 mangrove_j… Lutjanus argen… -19.3
10 lower_ro… 2026-05-03 00:00:00 flathe…     3 flathead    Platycephalus … -19.3
# ℹ 417 more rows
# ℹ 5 more variables: lon <dbl>, zone <fct>, mean_temp <dbl>,
#   mean_salinity <dbl>, mean_turbidity <dbl>

Note that we only have 427 rows of data…4 species, 4 sites, and 30 surveys = 4 * 4 * 30 = 480!

Some days there were no species counted at some sites, so these rows were omitted from the data. We need to wrangle the data so that zero counts are represented as 0 rather than NA. This is important when it comes time to data visualization because we want to be able to distinguish between true absenses (0) and missing data (NA).

6 Wrangle final dataset

Code 
catch_data_final |> 
  complete(
    nesting(site, date, 
           # lat, lon, 
            zone, mean_temp, mean_salinity, mean_turbidity), scientific_name
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Convert the resulting NA counts to 0
    count = coalesce(count, 0)
  )
# A tibble: 480 × 12
   site       date                zone    mean_temp mean_salinity mean_turbidity
   <chr>      <dttm>              <fct>       <dbl>         <dbl>          <dbl>
 1 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 Downst…      23.8          28.4           27.9
 2 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 Downst…      23.8          28.4           27.9
 3 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 Downst…      23.8          28.4           27.9
 4 lower_ross 2026-05-01 00:00:00 Downst…      23.8          28.4           27.9
 5 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 Downst…      24.0          27.7           24.1
 6 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 Downst…      24.0          27.7           24.1
 7 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 Downst…      24.0          27.7           24.1
 8 lower_ross 2026-05-02 00:00:00 Downst…      24.0          27.7           24.1
 9 lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 Downst…      24.2          28.1           20.6
10 lower_ross 2026-05-03 00:00:00 Downst…      24.2          28.1           20.6
# ℹ 470 more rows
# ℹ 6 more variables: scientific_name <chr>, species <chr>, count <dbl>,
#   common_name <chr>, lat <dbl>, lon <dbl>

We first want to check there is an actual difference in salinity among zones:

Code 
sal_sum <- catch_data_final |> 
  group_by(scientific_name, zone) |> 
  summarise(
    # se_salinity = sd(mean_salinity) / sqrt(n()),
    sd_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(sal_sum)
# A tibble: 16 × 4
   scientific_name           zone       sd_salinity median_salinity
   <chr>                     <fct>            <dbl>           <dbl>
 1 Acanthopagrus spp.        Upstream         0.196            4.98
 2 Acanthopagrus spp.        Middle           0.395           15.0 
 3 Acanthopagrus spp.        Downstream       0.386           27.9 
 4 Acanthopagrus spp.        Marine           0.107           34.0 
 5 Lates calcarifer          Upstream         0.191            4.99
 6 Lates calcarifer          Middle           0.412           15.0 
 7 Lates calcarifer          Downstream       0.361           27.9 
 8 Lates calcarifer          Marine           0.105           34.0 
 9 Lutjanus argentimaculatus Upstream         0.191            5.00
10 Lutjanus argentimaculatus Middle           0.387           15.0 
11 Lutjanus argentimaculatus Downstream       0.355           27.9 
12 Lutjanus argentimaculatus Marine           0.107           34.0 
13 Platycephalus spp.        Upstream         0.191            5.02
14 Platycephalus spp.        Middle           0.403           14.9 
15 Platycephalus spp.        Downstream       0.348           27.9 
16 Platycephalus spp.        Marine           0.104           34.0
Code 
# Or with plotrix
# library(plotrix)
# catch_data_final |> 
#   group_by(scientific_name, zone) |> 
#   summarise(
#     se_salinity = std.error(count),
#     # sd_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
#     median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
#     .groups = "drop"
#   )

6.1 Make a point and error plot

Code 
sal_sum |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_salinity)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_salinity - sd_salinity, ymax = median_salinity + sd_salinity), width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Salinity by Zone with Standard Deviation error bars",
       x = "Zone",
       y = "Median Salinity (± SD) (ppt)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

6.2 Now calculate mean and SE catch

Code 
fish_stats <- catch_data_final |> 
  group_by(zone, scientific_name) |> 
  summarise(
    mean_catch = mean(count),
    se_catch = sd(count) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
Code 
fish_stats |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = mean_catch, fill = zone)) +
  geom_line(group = 1) +
  # add points from master data
  geom_point(data = catch_data_final, aes(x = zone, y = count),
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.2), size = 1, alpha = 0.5, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_point(position = "dodge", size = 4, pch = 23) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_catch - se_catch, ymax = mean_catch + se_catch), 
                position = position_dodge(0.9), width = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~scientific_name, scales = "fixed") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "italic", size = 10, colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Predatory Fish Abundance across Estuarine Salinity Gradients",
       subtitle = "Ross River Estuarine System, May 2026",
       x = "Spatial Zone",
       y = "Mean Catch per Day (± SE)",
       fill = "Spatial Zone")
Source Code

    chunk_output_type: inline

# Introduction

Here we will be wrangling messy data files on water quality and predatory fish species from the Ross River estuary fish surveys. We know that there are 4 survey sites, 4 species, and 30 days of sampling. One of the files contains metadata on the sites and sensor data on the environmental conditions at each site.

# Housekeeping
```{r Housekeeping}
#| message: true
#| warning: false
rm(list = ls())
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  include = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE)
```

```{r Connect to GitHub, include=FALSE}
# in the console:
#   credentials::git_credential_ask()
#   usethis::git_sitrep()

# if error message comes up that default branch is origin when it should be "main", enter this in the console:
#   usethis::git_default_branch_rediscover()

# in the console:
#   usethis::use_git()
#   usethis::use_github()

# in RStudio terminal:
  # git remote add origin https://github.com/katiedyck/MB5370_Module02.git
  # git push -u origin main
  # git push
```

# Packages
```{r packages}
library(tidyverse)  # includes stringr, lubridate, ggplot2, dplyr, purrr, etc.
library(readxl)     # for reading .xlsx files
```

# Loading and Cleaning the data

## Load catch data

The catch data is divided across 4 sheets in one excel file.

Load sheets individually:

```{r load catch data}
catch1 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 1)
catch2 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 2)
catch3 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 3)
catch4 <- read_excel("../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx", sheet = 4)
```

```{r load catch day V2, echo=FALSE}
# Use `purrr` package to load "estuary_catch_log.xlsx" file and join all 4 sheets
# file_path <- "../data/workshop2/estuary_catch_log.xlsx"
# catch_data <- excel_sheets(file_path) |>
#     map_dfr(~read_excel(path = file_path, sheet = .x))
```

## Inspect individual sheets

```{r investigate catch data sheets}
#| code-fold: true

print(catch1)
print(catch2)
print(catch3)
print(catch4)
```
There are variations in the species and site names, but they otherwise look to be the same. We can clean after binding all the rows together.

## Bind rows together

```{r bind catch data rows}
catch_data <- bind_rows(catch1, catch2, catch3, catch4)
# Clean up the environment
rm(catch1, catch2, catch3, catch4)
```

## Examine catch data

```{r Examine catch data}
print(catch_data)
```

We want to know how many levels of `site` and `species` we have, which we can't tell how many there are from these outputs. One option is to check these columns explicitly using `distinct()`:

```{r unique species and sites}
#| code-fold: true

catch_data |> 
  distinct(site)

catch_data |> 
  distinct(species)
```

### Next steps for catch data

We need the site names to have a uniform format. It looks like sites correspond to unique zones on the Ross River, but we need to load the metadata to confirm this.
We can use the `stringr` package to make `site` names consistent so we can then load the taxonomic dictionary file to translate common to scientific species names.

## Load metadata

```{r load remaining datasets, echo=FALSE}
metadata <- read_csv("../data/workshop2/estuary_metadata.csv")
glimpse(metadata)
```

## Clean metadata

```{r check data tables}
#| code-fold: true

metadata |> 
  distinct(site_name)
```

Site names in `metadata` are all over the place. We need to standardize them so we can join this table with the catch data table.

### Standardize site names in catch data and metadata

Aside from simply standardizing the site names, we also want to convert the character variables to factors.

```{r clean catch_data site names}
catch_data <- catch_data |> 
  mutate(
        # Replace spaces with underscores
        site = str_replace_all(site, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
        # Change text to lowercase
        site = str_to_lower(site)
    ) |> 
  # Convert all character to factor
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))
```

```{r clean metadata site names}
metadata <- metadata |> 
  mutate(
        # Replace spaces with underscores
        site_name = str_replace_all(site_name, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
        # Change text to lowercase
        site_name = str_to_lower(site_name)
    ) |> 
  # Convert all character to factor
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as_factor))
```

### Recheck site names

```{r recheck site names}
#| code-fold: true

catch_data |> 
  distinct(site)

metadata |> 
  distinct(site_name)
```

Site names are now standardized across the catch data and metadata files.

## Load taxonomic dictionary

```{r load dictionary data, echo=FALSE}
dictionary <- read_csv("../data/workshop2/species_dictionary.csv")
glimpse(dictionary)
```
## Standardize species (common) names in catch data

The common species names are already in a standardized format. We need to standardize the species names in the catch data to match. 

```{r fix catch data species names}
#| code-fold: true

catch_data <- catch_data |>
  mutate(
    species = str_to_lower(str_trim(species)),
    species = str_replace_all(species, pattern = " ", replacement = "_")
  )

catch_data |> 
  distinct(species)
```

### Create new variable ("scientific_name") in catch log by joining with taxonomic dictionary 
```{r join-species-dict}
#| code-fold: true

catch_data <- catch_data |>
  mutate(common_name = species) |>
  left_join(dictionary, by = join_by(common_name))
```

```{r}
glimpse(catch_data)
```

Looks good - the species and site names are fixed.

## Load and wrangle sensor data

```{r load sensor data, echo=FALSE}
sensor_raw <- read_csv("../data/workshop2/estuary_sonde_data.csv")
str(sensor_raw)
```

- Sonde data was collected every 15 minutes for the same 4 sites across the 30 days of sampling. The data was sourced in a daily format, and outliers were removed.
  - `timestamp` is a character-type column. We will probably want to parse this to a date or date-time column.
- There are three different environmental variables measured: *temperature*, *salinity*, and *turbidity*.
  - Environmental variables need cleaning: some negative salinity values, some -999 turbidity values (failed values).
- **Could investigate effects of all three on fish spp abundance or pick one? Need to think about prior research and hypotheses etc.**


## Wrangle sensor data

```{r clean sensor data}
#| code-fold: true

sensor_data <- sensor_raw |> 
    mutate(
      # Parse the character string to Day/Month/Year Hour:Minute
      datetime = dmy_hm(timestamp),
      # Chance -999.0 to NA values
      turbidity = na_if(turbidity, -999),
      # Convert negative salinity values to NA
      salinity = ifelse(salinity < 0, NA, salinity)
    )

print(sensor_data)
```

### Summarize 15-minute sensor data into daily means

```{r summ 15min sensor data}
#| code-fold: true

sensor_daily <- sensor_data |>
  mutate(date = as_date(floor_date(datetime, "day"))) |>
  group_by(site, date) |>
  summarise(
    mean_temp = mean(temperature, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_salinity = mean(salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_turbidity = mean(turbidity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop")

print(sensor_daily)
```

# Join catch data with metadata and sensor data

```{r join catch data with metadata and sensor data}
catch_data_final <- catch_data |> 
  left_join(metadata, by = join_by(site == site_name)) |> 
  left_join(sensor_daily, by = join_by(site, date))

print(catch_data_final)
```

Note that we only have 427 rows of data...4 species, 4 sites, and 30 surveys = 4 * 4 * 30 = **480**!

Some days there were no species counted at some sites, so these rows were omitted from the data. We need to wrangle the data so that zero counts are represented as 0 rather than NA. This is important when it comes time to data visualization because we want to be able to distinguish between true absenses (0) and missing data (NA).

# Wrangle final dataset

```{r built zero-catch framework}
#| code-fold: true

catch_data_final |> 
  complete(
    nesting(site, date, 
           # lat, lon, 
            zone, mean_temp, mean_salinity, mean_turbidity), scientific_name
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Convert the resulting NA counts to 0
    count = coalesce(count, 0)
  )
```

We first want to check there is an actual difference in salinity among zones:

```{r mean/sd/se salinity}
sal_sum <- catch_data_final |> 
  group_by(scientific_name, zone) |> 
  summarise(
    # se_salinity = sd(mean_salinity) / sqrt(n()),
    sd_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

print(sal_sum)

# Or with plotrix
# library(plotrix)
# catch_data_final |> 
#   group_by(scientific_name, zone) |> 
#   summarise(
#     se_salinity = std.error(count),
#     # sd_salinity = sd(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
#     median_salinity = median(mean_salinity, na.rm = TRUE),
#     .groups = "drop"
#   )
```

## Make a point and error plot
```{r salinity summary plot}
sal_sum |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = median_salinity)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = median_salinity - sd_salinity, ymax = median_salinity + sd_salinity), width = 0.2) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Median Salinity by Zone with Standard Deviation error bars",
       x = "Zone",
       y = "Median Salinity (± SD) (ppt)") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
```

## Now calculate mean and SE catch
```{r}
fish_stats <- catch_data_final |> 
  group_by(zone, scientific_name) |> 
  summarise(
    mean_catch = mean(count),
    se_catch = sd(count) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
```

```{r fish catch summary plot}
fish_stats |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = zone, y = mean_catch, fill = zone)) +
  geom_line(group = 1) +
  # add points from master data
  geom_point(data = catch_data_final, aes(x = zone, y = count),
             position = position_jitter(width = 0.2), size = 1, alpha = 0.5, inherit.aes = FALSE) +
  geom_point(position = "dodge", size = 4, pch = 23) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_catch - se_catch, ymax = mean_catch + se_catch), 
                position = position_dodge(0.9), width = 0.2) +
  facet_wrap(~scientific_name, scales = "fixed") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    strip.text = element_text(face = "italic", size = 10, colour = "grey20"),
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Predatory Fish Abundance across Estuarine Salinity Gradients",
       subtitle = "Ross River Estuarine System, May 2026",
       x = "Spatial Zone",
       y = "Mean Catch per Day (± SE)",
       fill = "Spatial Zone")
```
